meta.version: mb-vectors 1.3 — thermo core + core modules + reaction-engineering + reformer modules, vectors MB-001..MB-062
meta.target: api/_lib/mb-engine.js (mb-engine 0.8.x, parts 1+2+3+4 — thermo core + module solvers + cstr/pfr/pfr-recycle + smr/atr)
meta.units: T [K], P [bar(a)], molar h [kJ/mol], Cp [J/mol·K], stream H [kJ/h]
meta.notes: Expected values are EXTERNAL references (NIST WebBook, IAPWS-IF97 steam tables, SVN App. C, CRC/Perry class values) — never engine output — so these vectors detect coefficient transcription errors, not just regressions. Groups: A gas Cp/H (Shomate+SVN), B water/IF97, C Watson latent heat, D phase determination edge cases, E formation-basis reaction consistency, F solveT_forH round-trips. Group G (MB-026..MB-040): module solvers via runModule — mixer, splitter, flash (Rachford-Rice), heat-exchanger, rotating (pump/compressor/turbine), conversion reactor; expected values are hand calculations from steam tables, published Antoine constants, ideal-gas relations, and Felder & Rousseau-class worked figures — never engine output. Group H (MB-041..MB-052): cstr / pfr / pfr-recycle — expected values are hand calculations from the standard ideal-reactor design equations (Fogler, Elements of Chemical Reaction Engineering; Levenspiel, Chemical Reaction Engineering): CSTR X = Da/(1+Da) family, PFR X = 1−e^(−Da) family, recycle-reactor closed form X_ov = 1 − E/(1+R−R·E) with E = e^(−Da0/(1+R)) — never engine output. Test chemistry is the mass-balanced model liquid reaction 2 C3H6(l) → C6H12(l) (cyclohexane; 2·42.081 = 84.162 vs 84.161 kg/kmol, closure −0.0012 % — deliberately visible in closure_pct) with pseudo-nth-order rate laws in C3H6; feed 520 kg/h pure C3H6 with params.rho_kgm3 = 520 gives v = 1 m3/h and Ca0 = 12.35712 kmol/m3 exactly. Group I (MB-053..MB-062): smr / atr reformer equilibria — expected values are INDEPENDENT hand calculations (Python, separate from the engine) of the simultaneous CH4+H2O⇌CO+3H2 and CO+H2O⇌CO2+H2 equilibria from the published K correlations the spec prescribes (Twigg, Catalyst Handbook: lnK1 = 30.114 − 26830/T [bar²]; Moe 1962: lnK2 = 4400/T − 4.036), with adiabatic balances closed on NIST WebBook Shomate enthalpies — never engine output — and each point is cross-anchored to a published reformer equilibrium chart/table figure named in its source field. Dry-composition tolerance 0.015 mole fraction (1.5 % absolute per spec); adiabatic T_out ±25 K covers the ideal-gas-steam hand calc vs the engine’s IF97 water route.

vectors.desc: Shomate Cp: CO2 gas at 298.15 K
vectors.source: NIST WebBook CO2 gas, Cp(298.15 K) = 37.12 J/mol·K

vectors.desc: Shomate enthalpy delta: N2 H(1000 K) − H(298.15 K)
vectors.source: NIST WebBook N2, H°(1000)−H°(298.15) = 21.463 kJ/mol

vectors.desc: Shomate enthalpy delta: CO2 H(1000 K) − H(298.15 K)
vectors.source: NIST WebBook CO2, H°(1000)−H°(298.15) = 33.40 kJ/mol

vectors.desc: Formation basis: CO2 gas h(298.15 K) equals Hf° exactly (Shomate delta = 0 at Tref)
vectors.source: NIST WebBook, ΔfH°(CO2, g) = −393.52 kJ/mol

vectors.desc: SVN polynomial Cp: ethane gas at 400 K (no Shomate set — must route via cp_svn)
vectors.source: SVN App. C Table C.1 ethane; handbook Cp(400 K) ≈ 65.5–66 J/mol·K

vectors.desc: SVN enthalpy delta: propane H(400 K) − H(298.15 K)
vectors.source: SVN Table C.1 integral; NIST-derived ΔH(298→400) ≈ 8.6 kJ/mol

vectors.desc: IF97 anchor: liquid water at 298.15 K / 1 bar equals Hf°(liq) on the formation basis
vectors.source: NIST, ΔfH°(H2O, l) = −285.83 kJ/mol; IF97 delta ≡ 0 at the 298.15 K anchor

vectors.desc: IF97 saturated liquid at 373.15 K / 1.01325 bar (region-4 point, liquid branch)
IF97 superheated steam at 423.15 K / 1 bar (region 2)
IF97 latent heat at 373.15 K from the gas/liquid branch difference
vectors.source: steam tables hfg(100 °C) = 2256.5 kJ/kg × 0.018015 kg/mol = 40.66 kJ/mol

vectors.desc: IF97 estimated ice branch: water at 263.15 K sits below hf_liq − ΔHfus
Watson anchor identity: ΔHvap(H2O) at Tb returns the stored anchor exactly
vectors.source: NIST, ΔHvap(H2O, 373.15 K) = 40.65 kJ/mol — at T = Tref the Watson ratio is 1

vectors.desc: Watson extrapolation down: ΔHvap(H2O) at 298.15 K vs calorimetric value
vectors.source: NIST, ΔHvap(H2O, 298.15 K) = 43.99 kJ/mol; Watson 0.38-exponent gives ≈44.6 (correlation error < 1.5%)

vectors.desc: Watson with dhvap_ref_K override: CO2 at 250 K anchored at the TRIPLE point (216.59 K), not the sublimation tb_K
vectors.source: Watson from ΔHvap(216.59 K) = 15.3 kJ/mol; handbook ΔHvap(250 K) ≈ 12.3–13 kJ/mol. Guards the dhvap_ref_K path.

vectors.desc: Phase at normal Tb: water at 373.15 K / 1.00 bar is GAS (Tsat(1 bar) = 372.76 K — 0.4 K superheated)
vectors.source: IF97: Tsat(1.0 bar) = 372.756 K; the classic at-Tb trap — 100 °C at 1 bar (not 1 atm) is already vapor

vectors.desc: Phase just below saturation: water at 372.0 K / 1.00 bar is LIQUID
vectors.source: IF97: 372.0 K < Tsat(1.0 bar) = 372.756 K → region 1 liquid

vectors.desc: Phase above Tc: propane at 400 K / 50 bar is GAS regardless of pressure (Tc = 369.8 K)
vectors.source: propane Tc = 369.8 K < 400 K — supercritical rule fires before any Psat comparison

vectors.desc: Nonvolatile solid: NaCl at 298.15 K / 1 bar is SOLID with psat = 0
vectors.source: spec: nonvolatile flag → solid below Tm (1073.8 K), psat pinned to 0

vectors.desc: Nonvolatile melt: NaCl at 1200 K / 1 bar is LIQUID (above Tm 1073.8 K), still nonvolatile
vectors.source: spec: nonvolatile + T ≥ Tm → liquid; never gas (tb_K = 1738 K irrelevant while flagged)

vectors.desc: Antoine Psat route: ethanol at 350 K / 1.01325 bar is LIQUID (Psat ≈ 0.96 bar just under P)
vectors.source: Antoine (CRC set): Psat(350 K) ≈ 0.95–0.97 bar < 1.01325 bar; Tb = 351.44 K — 1.4 K below the boil

vectors.desc: Formation-basis element identity: H2 gas at 298.15 K has h = 0 (element reference state)
vectors.source: ΔfH°(element, standard state) ≡ 0; Shomate H(298.15)−H(298.15) ≡ 0 by the F−H closed form

vectors.desc: Formation-basis reaction consistency: H2 + ½O2 → H2O(g) at 298.15 K from stream enthalpies alone
vectors.source: NIST, ΔfH°(H2O, g) = −241.83 kJ/mol. Proves reaction heat is implicit in h — including the IF97-routed water branch.

vectors.desc: Formation-basis reaction consistency: CH4 + 2O2 → CO2 + 2H2O(g), ΔH°rxn(298) = −802.3 kJ/mol (LHV check)
vectors.source: Hess: −393.52 + 2(−241.83) − (−74.87) = −802.31 kJ/mol (methane lower heating value)

vectors.desc: solveT_forH round-trip: pure liquid water stream, T_true = 350 K over bracket [280, 420]
vectors.source: self-consistency: streamEnthalpy(T) → solveT_forH(H) must invert on the IF97 liquid branch

vectors.desc: solveT_forH round-trip: air (N2/O2 Shomate gas mix), T_true = 800 K over bracket [300, 1500]
vectors.source: self-consistency on the multi-component Shomate gas branch (crosses both N2 ranges at 500 K)

vectors.desc: mixer: adiabatic 50/50 hot/cold water — T_out within 0.1 K of hand calc
vectors.source: Steam-table hand calc: hf(90 °C)=376.97, hf(20 °C)=83.92 kJ/kg → mixed h=230.44 → T=55.04 °C=328.19 K (constant-cp hand check 328.15 K)

vectors.desc: mixer (Felder-style worked example): quench 100 kg/h saturated steam with 900 kg/h water at 20 °C
vectors.source: Felder & Rousseau-class steam-table energy balance (ch. 8 style): hg(1 atm)=2676.0, hf(20 °C)=83.92 kJ/kg → h_mix=343.13 kJ/kg → T≈81.95 °C=355.10 K

vectors.desc: splitter: 60/40 split — identical T/composition, exact closure, zero duty
vectors.source: Definition of a splitter: mass split with unchanged intensive state — exact by construction

vectors.desc: flash: equimolar benzene/toluene at 95 °C, 1 atm — Rachford-Rice V/F and vapor composition
vectors.source: Raoult/Rachford-Rice hand calc with published Antoine constants (Lange class): Psat(95 °C) benzene 1.569 bar, toluene 0.636 bar → K=1.548/0.627 → ψ=0.4305, y_benzene=0.6263

vectors.desc: flash: equimolar benzene/toluene at 110 °C, 1 atm — above dew point, all-vapor detection
vectors.source: Antoine hand calc: K(110 °C)=2.31 (benzene) / 0.98 (toluene) → Σz(K−1)/K=+0.27>0 — feed is superheated vapor (dew point ≈ 98.8 °C at 1 atm)

vectors.desc: flash: water/NaCl (z=0.9/0.1 mol) at 120 °C, 1 bar — nonvolatile salt stays in the liquid, salt-free vapor
vectors.source: Rachford-Rice hand calc with steam-table Psat(120 °C)=1.9854 bar → K_w=1.985, K_NaCl=0 → ψ=0.7985; nonvolatile → y_NaCl=0 exactly

vectors.desc: heat-exchanger both_T: water/water 80→60 °C vs 20→40 °C, counter-current — duty and LMTD=40 K
vectors.source: Steam tables: hf(80)−hf(60)=334.95−251.18=83.77 kJ/kg → 23.27 kW at 1000 kg/h; equal 40 K approaches at both ends → LMTD=40 K exactly

vectors.desc: heat-exchanger solve_cold: hot water 90→50 °C (1000 kg/h) heats 2000 kg/h from 20 °C — cold outlet T
heat-exchanger both_T: 100→60 °C vs 30→50 °C counter-current — classic unequal-end LMTD
rotating/pump: 10 000 kg/h water, ΔP=5 bar, η=0.7 — hydraulic + shaft power and friction T rise
vectors.source: Hand calc: W_hyd=ṁΔP/ρ=(2.778 kg/s·5·10⁵ Pa)/998=1.392 kW; shaft=1.988 kW; ΔT=ΔP(1/η−1)/(ρcp)=0.051 K

vectors.desc: rotating/compressor: 100 kg/h argon 1→10 bar from 25 °C, η=1 — exact monatomic isentropic relation
vectors.source: Ideal-gas hand calc, γ=5/3 (monatomic, NIST cp=20.786 J/mol·K): T2s=298.15·10^0.4=748.9 K; W=cpΔT·n=20.786·450.8·2.503 kmol/h=6.515 kW

vectors.desc: rotating/turbine: 100 kg/h argon 10→1 bar from 800 K, η=0.8 — isentropic then efficiency, W negative
reactor (Felder worked example): C3H8+5O2→3CO2+4H2O, X=1, isothermal 25 °C — dHr298 equals the tabulated heat of combustion (HHV) and Q follows from formation-basis enthalpies
reactor adiabatic: CH4 + theoretical air (O2:N2 = 2:7.52), X=1 — adiabatic flame temperature, no dissociation
vectors.source: Çengel & Boles (Thermodynamics) worked example class: theoretical adiabatic flame temperature of CH4 with stoichiometric air, complete combustion, no dissociation ≈ 2330 K (variable ideal-gas cp)

vectors.desc: reactor sequential: SMR (X_CH4=0.8) then WGS (X_CO=0.5) at 800 °C — hand-tracked mole table and near-exact mass closure
vectors.source: Hand mole table, basis 1 kmol/h CH4 + 3 H2O: ξ1=0.8 → CH4 0.2, H2O 2.2, CO 0.8, H2 2.4; ξ2=0.5·0.8=0.4 → CO 0.4, H2O 1.8, CO2 0.4, H2 2.8; atomic MWs make closure exact to <0.001%

vectors.desc: cstr: first-order liquid, V→X — Da = kτ = 2 → X = 2/3 (Fogler CSTR design eq.)
vectors.source: Fogler (Elements of CRE) first-order liquid CSTR: X = Da/(1+Da), Da = kτ = (2/7200 s⁻¹)(7200 s) = 2 → X = 2/3; Ca0 = 520/42.081 = 12.35712 kmol/m³. Hand calculation.

vectors.desc: cstr: second-order liquid, X→V — τ = X/(k·Ca0·(1−X)²) = 16185 s, V = 4.4958 m³; batch-time equivalent 3237 s
vectors.source: Fogler CSTR design eq., 2nd order liquid: τ = X/(k·Ca0·(1−X)²) = 0.8/(1e-4 · 12.35712 · 0.04) = 16185 s → V = τ·v = 4.49583 m³; Da₂ = kτCa0 = 20. Batch/PFR equivalent t = X/(k·Ca0·(1−X)) = 3237 s. Hand calculation.

vectors.desc: cstr: Arrhenius k(A=1e5 s⁻¹, Ea=50 kJ/mol, T=350 K) = 3.4519e-3 s⁻¹ → Da = 12.427, X = 0.92552 (40 bar keeps C3H6 liquid)
vectors.source: Arrhenius: k = A·e^(−Ea/RT) = 1e5·exp(−50/(0.008314462·350)) = 3.45186e-3 s⁻¹; τ = 3600 s → Da = 12.4267; first-order CSTR X = Da/(1+Da) = 0.925522 (Fogler). Hand calculation.

vectors.desc: cstr: adiabatic + Arrhenius (dilute 5 % C3H6 in cyclohexane) — formation-basis identity Q ≈ 0 with converged T↔X loop
vectors.source: Formation-basis adiabatic identity: with T_out solved from stream enthalpies, the reported duty Q = Hout − Hin must return ≈ 0 (reaction heat is implicit in the formation enthalpies); exothermic-CSTR T↔k(T)↔X damped loop must converge.

vectors.desc: pfr: first-order liquid, V→X — X = 1 − e^(−kτ) = 1 − e⁻² = 0.86466 (exact per-segment march)
vectors.source: Levenspiel / Fogler PFR design eq., first order constant density: X = 1 − e^(−kτ) = 1 − e⁻² = 0.864665. Hand calculation.

vectors.desc: pfr: second-order liquid, X→V — kτCa0 = X/(1−X) = 4 → τ = 3237 s, V = 0.89917 m³
vectors.source: Fogler PFR design eq., 2nd order liquid: kτCa0 = X/(1−X) = 4 → τ = 4/(1e-4 · 12.35712) = 3237 s, V = 0.899167 m³; PFR ≡ constant-volume batch, so the batch-time equivalent equals τ. Hand calculation.

vectors.desc: pfr: zero-order liquid — X = kτ/Ca0 = (1e-3 · 5040)/12.35712 = 0.40786
vectors.source: Levenspiel zero-order design eq. (identical for PFR and batch): X = k·τ/Ca0 = 1e-3 · 5040 / 12.35712 = 0.407862. Hand calculation.

vectors.desc: pfr: axial profile — first-order X(z) = 1 − e^(−Da·z); at z = 0.5 (profile index 25 of 50), X = 1 − e⁻¹ = 0.63212
vectors.source: First-order PFR axial profile X(z) = 1 − e^(−Da·z), Da = 2: X(0.5) = 1 − e⁻¹ = 0.632121, X(1) = 1 − e⁻² = 0.864665. The exact-per-segment march must reproduce the closed form at every profile node.

vectors.desc: pfr-recycle: R = 50 approaches the CSTR limit — closed form X_ov = 1 − E/(1+R−R·E) = 0.67102 (CSTR: 2/3)
vectors.source: Recycle-reactor closed form (Fogler Ch. 6 / Levenspiel recycle reactor), first order: X_ov = 1 − E/(1+R−R·E), E = e^(−Da0/(1+R)), Da0 = kV/v_fresh = 2; R = 50 → X_ov = 0.671024, X_sp = 1 − E = 0.038457 — approaching the CSTR limit Da/(1+Da) = 2/3 as R → ∞. Iterations expected ≤ 30 with bounded Wegstein.

vectors.desc: pfr-recycle: R = 0 degenerates to a single PFR pass — X_ov = X_sp = 1 − e⁻²
vectors.source: Levenspiel recycle-reactor limit R → 0: plug flow, X = 1 − e^(−Da0) = 1 − e⁻² = 0.864665; single pass, no tear iteration.

vectors.desc: pfr-recycle: Wegstein-vs-analytic identity, R = 1, Da0 = 2 — tear fixed point must equal the first-order closed form
vectors.source: First-order recycle identity: X_ov = 1 − E/(1+R−R·E), E = e^(−Da0/(1+R)); R = 1, Da0 = 2 → E = e⁻¹, X_ov = 1 − e⁻¹/(2−e⁻¹) = 0.774600 and X_sp = 1 − e⁻¹ = 0.632121. The Wegstein-converged tear must land on the analytic fixed point (bounded q ∈ [−5,0]).

vectors.desc: pfr-recycle: adiabatic loop (dilute exothermic feed, R = 2) — envelope identity Q ≈ 0 at the converged tear incl. temperature
vectors.source: Adiabatic recycle envelope identity on the formation basis: mixing point and reactor both adiabatic → fresh-in / product-out duty must return ≈ 0 at the converged tear. Exercises the Wegstein tear including its temperature variable; iterations expected ≤ 30.

vectors.desc: smr fired: pure CH4, S/C = 3, 20 bar, 800 °C, ATE 0/0 — simultaneous reformer + shift equilibrium, dry composition, H2/CO, CH4 slip, extents
vectors.source: Hand equilibrium calculation (independent Python solve, not engine output) from the published correlations lnK1 = 30.114 − 26830/T [bar²] (Twigg, Catalyst Handbook, 2nd ed., App.) and lnK2 = 4400/T − 4.036 (Moe, Chem. Eng. Prog. 58(3), 1962). Conditions of Speight fig. 7-3 (steam reforming equilibrium at 20 atm, H2O/CH4 = 3): dry CH4 slip ≈ 7.6 % at 800 °C, matching the published chart.

vectors.desc: smr fired: pure CH4, S/C = 3, 20 bar, 1000 °C, ATE 0/0 — near-complete CH4 conversion (published charts: conversion “complete at about 1,000 °C” at 20 atm)
vectors.source: Hand equilibrium calculation from Twigg lnK1 / Moe lnK2 (see MB-053). Anchor: Speight fig. 7-3 (20 atm, H2O/CH4 = 3) — “methane conversion is complete at about 1,000 °C”; dry slip 0.43 % reproduces the chart’s end point.

vectors.desc: smr fired: CH4 + 2 H2O, 1 bar, 1300 K, ATE 0/0 — SVN Ex. 13.14 conditions: reforming essentially complete, product split set by the shift equilibrium
vectors.source: Hand equilibrium calculation from Twigg lnK1 / Moe lnK2 (see MB-053) at the conditions of Smith, Van Ness & Abbott Ex. 13.14 (industrial CH4 reforming, 1 bar, 1300 K, 2 mol steam per mol CH4), where SVN’s ΔG°-based solution likewise gives near-total CH4 conversion with the mixture governed by the water-gas-shift equilibrium.

vectors.desc: smr fired: pure CH4, S/C = 3.5, 30 bar, 800 °C, ATE 0/0 — ammonia-plant primary-reformer point: ≈ 9 % dry CH4 slip
vectors.source: Hand equilibrium calculation from Twigg lnK1 / Moe lnK2 (see MB-053). Anchor: published ammonia-plant primary-reformer outlets at ~30 bar / ~800 °C / S/C 3–4 run ≈ 9–10 % dry CH4 (Twigg, Catalyst Handbook, steam-reforming ch.; Appl, “Ammonia”, Ullmann’s Encyclopedia).

vectors.desc: smr fired: MB-053 conditions with approach-to-equilibrium ATE_smr = 20 K, ATE_wgs = 10 K — slip drops as the reformer equilibrium is evaluated hotter
vectors.source: Hand equilibrium calculation from Twigg lnK1 evaluated at T+20 K and Moe lnK2 at T+10 K — the approach-to-equilibrium convention for reformer rating (Twigg, Catalyst Handbook: reformer duty expressed as an ATE on the reforming reaction).

vectors.desc: smr fired: pure CH4, S/C = 3, 20 bar, 700 °C, ATE 0/0 — the dry-CO2 maximum region of the published equilibrium chart
vectors.source: Hand equilibrium calculation from Twigg lnK1 / Moe lnK2 (see MB-053). Anchor: Speight fig. 7-3 (20 atm, H2O/CH4 = 3) — “the production of CO2 from the water gas shift reaction is maximum around 700 °C”; the 11.6 % dry CO2 here sits at that published maximum.

vectors.desc: smr secondary: primary-reformer effluent (30 bar, 800 °C) + preheated air (600 °C), O2 fully burned against CH4 then equilibrium, adiabatic T_out by outer bisection — ammonia-plant secondary-reformer point
vectors.source: Hand calculation: CH4 + 2 O2 → CO2 + 2 H2O on all fed O2, then Twigg/Moe equilibrium with the adiabatic balance closed on NIST WebBook Shomate H(T)−H(298)+Hf (independent Python solve). Anchor: published ammonia-plant secondary reformers leave < 0.5 % dry CH4 with ~23 % N2 at ≈ 1000–1050 °C outlet (Appl, “Ammonia”, Ullmann’s; Twigg, Catalyst Handbook). T tolerance ±25 K covers ideal-gas-steam hand calc vs the engine’s IF97 water route on the wet feed.

vectors.desc: atr: pure CH4 at 650 K / 25 bar, S/C = 0.6, O2/C = 0.6 (pure O2 at 298 K, steam at 650 K), adiabatic — GTL-class syngas with H2/CO ≈ 2.3
vectors.source: Hand calculation (independent Python solve): full O2 combustion of CH4, then Twigg/Moe equilibrium with the adiabatic balance on NIST Shomate enthalpies. Anchor: published ATR operation at O2/C ≈ 0.6 and low S/C delivers H2/CO ≈ 2.0–2.3 at ≈ 1020–1050 °C outlet (Aasberg-Petersen et al., J. Nat. Gas Sci. Eng. 3 (2011) 423–459, Topsøe ATR).

vectors.desc: atr with AIR oxidant: pure CH4 at 650 K / 20 bar, S/C = 1.0, O2/C = 0.55, air and steam at 650 K, adiabatic — nitrogen-diluted syngas
vectors.source: Hand calculation (independent Python solve): air taken as 21 % O2 / 79 % N2, full O2 combustion of CH4, then Twigg/Moe equilibrium with the adiabatic balance on NIST Shomate enthalpies. Anchor: air-blown ATR/secondary-reforming trains show exactly this N2-diluted syngas pattern (~40 % dry N2) at ≈ 800–850 °C (Aasberg-Petersen et al. 2011; Appl, “Ammonia”, Ullmann’s).

vectors.desc: smr fired, natural-gas-like feed: 0.90 CH4 / 0.06 C2H6 / 0.03 C3H8 / 0.01 nC4H10, S/C = 3.2 (per mol C), 25 bar, 800 °C, default ATE (0 / +10 K) — full C2+ pre-crack then equilibrium
vectors.source: Hand calculation (independent Python solve): CnH2n+2 + n H2O → n CO + (2n+1) H2 applied fully to C2H6/C3H8/nC4H10 (Rostrup-Nielsen: higher hydrocarbons crack irreversibly to C1 species over the reformer inlet), then Twigg/Moe equilibrium at 1073.15 K (R1) / 1083.15 K (R2, default +10 K ATE), 25 bar.
